title: Introduction and Background
text: Automated literature review systems integrate natural language processing with retrieval-augmented generation to streamline knowledge synthesis—a process traditionally demanding substantial human effort. These systems address the challenge of automating discrete stages of the literature review workflow, including citation screening, information extraction, and claim synthesis, while preserving accuracy sufficient to reduce manual burden. The current literature presents three dominant methodological families: pure retrieval-augmented generation architectures leveraging large language models with external knowledge bases; traditional NLP pipeline approaches relying on classification and extraction models; and hybrid systems combining both paradigms. The systems examined in this review address distinct tasks within the broader literature review process, spanning initial screening phases through final synthesis generation. This comparative analysis examines how technical architectural choices influence system performance, evaluation rigor, and practical applicability across scholarly domains. The review synthesizes evidence from three representative systems to characterize the state of the field, with particular attention to identified gaps in multilingual evaluation, metric standardization, and evidence verification practices [p1, p2, p3].

title: Methodological Approaches
text: Automated literature review systems align with three distinct architectural paradigms, each exhibiting characteristic strengths and limitations. Retrieval-augmented generation approaches, exemplified by p2, employ large language models conditioned on retrieved document contexts to generate synthesis outputs. This architecture enables dynamic grounding in source materials but introduces dependence on underlying LLM quality. Pure RAG systems such as p2 operate primarily on structured knowledge bases like arXiv, leveraging retrieval mechanisms to surface relevant evidence before generation. Traditional NLP pipeline approaches, represented by p3, decompose the literature review task into discrete stages: citation classification, relation extraction, and information aggregation. The NLP pipeline family, exemplified by p3's PubMed-based citation screening system, demonstrates particular strength in structured evaluation scenarios where labeled corpora enable supervised classification. Hybrid architectures combining NLP extraction with LLM retrieval represent the third paradigm, as seen in p1, which claims superior coverage over keyword baselines through the integration of extracted entities with generative retrieval. The divergence between these families carries implications for task suitability: pipeline approaches offer interpretability and controlled performance on specific subtasks, while RAG-based systems provide more flexible synthesis capabilities at the cost of reduced determinism. Architectural choice fundamentally shapes evaluation possibilities and the performance claims that can be substantiated [p1, p2, p3].

title: Performance Metrics and Reporting Heterogeneity
text: The evaluation metrics employed across automated literature review systems exhibit substantial heterogeneity, impeding meaningful cross-study performance comparison. The three systems examined employ overlapping but non-identical evaluation frameworks: p1 reports ROUGE-L and F1 scores; p2 utilizes F1 and precision; and p3 combines F1 with AUC. Although F1 appears consistently across all three studies, calculation methodologies diverge substantially—ROUGE-L measures n-gram overlap at the token level, F1 balances precision and recall in classification contexts, and AUC assesses ranking quality across threshold values. This metric diversity reflects the varied tasks each system targets, ranging from generation quality assessment to retrieval ranking to classification performance, but creates a fundamental barrier to direct comparison. p1's claim of higher coverage than keyword baselines rests on ROUGE-L evaluation against a custom corpus, while p3's assertion that traditional NLP achieves competitive F1 but lags LLM on recall compares two different methodological families on the same PubMed dataset. The absence of standardized evaluation protocols means reported metrics may not capture identical phenomena, and performance differences attributed to architectural choices may instead reflect metric heterogeneity. This gap in metric standardization prevents the field from establishing reliable performance hierarchies or identifying which architectural decisions consistently yield improvements across evaluation conditions [p1, p2, p3].

title: Evaluation Scope and Cross-Lingual Generalization
text: Current automated literature review systems exhibit pronounced English-language dominance in evaluation, fundamentally limiting understanding of cross-lingual generalization capabilities. Among the three systems examined, p1 explicitly notes an English-only limitation, while p2 and p3 evaluate exclusively on English-language corpora—arXiv and PubMed, respectively—without reporting multilingual or cross-lingual experiments. This concentration on English-language evaluation data reflects the accessibility of large English scholarly repositories but leaves critical questions unanswered regarding system transferability to non-English research contexts. Systems demonstrating strong performance on English-language literature review tasks—including citation screening on PubMed abstracts or synthesis generation from arXiv papers—may exhibit substantially different behavior when applied to Chinese, Spanish, or other language scholarly corpora. No evidence currently exists to support claims of cross-lingual generalization, and methodological architectures may encode language-specific assumptions in tokenization, embedding spaces, or retrieval heuristics. This gap is particularly significant given that the majority of global scholarly output is non-English, and systems intended to accelerate literature review for international research communities cannot be confidently deployed beyond their evaluation languages without empirical validation of cross-lingual performance [p1, p2, p3].

title: Evidence Grounding and Claim Verification
text: The relationship between system performance claims and the evidence presented to substantiate them varies considerably across reviewed systems, with significant implications for claim reliability. p2 articulates a methodological claim that retrieval augmentation grounds claims in source evidence, positioning evidence traceability as a core architectural feature. This claim suggests that the RAG architecture enables explicit citation of source passages supporting generated outputs, theoretically enabling verification of synthesis claims against primary literature. However, the evidence chunk identifiers referenced do not include actual citation data or example verification chains, raising questions about whether the grounding capability has been empirically validated or remains architectural potential. p1's performance claim regarding higher coverage than keyword baselines references evidence but provides no documented comparison protocol or statistical significance testing to substantiate the coverage assertion. p3's claim about competitive F1 with lower LLM recall represents the most evidence-grounded assertion, as it specifies a direct comparison on the same PubMed dataset enabling independent verification. The broader pattern indicates that systems frequently assert performance advantages without providing verifiable evidence chains allowing readers to confirm or replicate reported findings. This limitation undermines scientific reproducibility and makes it difficult to distinguish genuine performance improvements from marketing-driven claims, particularly as systems become commercially deployed [p1, p2, p3].

title: Open Challenges and Future Directions
text: This comparative analysis reveals three interconnected challenges constraining advancement of reproducible and globally applicable literature review automation. First, English-language dominance in system evaluation must be addressed through systematic multilingual evaluation protocols assessing cross-lingual generalization rather than treating English performance as a proxy for universal capability. This requires development of standardized non-English evaluation corpora spanning multiple language families and scholarly domains. Second, metric reporting heterogeneity demands establishment of evaluation standards specifying which metrics are appropriate for which task types and requiring consistent calculation methodologies across studies. The current situation, where ROUGE-L, F1, precision, and AUC are reported without clear task-metric alignment, renders cross-study comparison unreliable and slows cumulative progress. Third, limited evidence grounding can be remedied through mandatory disclosure of evaluation protocols, statistical significance testing, and example verification chains connecting performance claims to specific experimental outcomes. Addressing these gaps would enable the field to move beyond isolated system descriptions toward cumulative knowledge about which architectural choices yield consistent improvements. The priority for future research should be development of benchmark evaluation frameworks incorporating multilingual coverage, standardized metrics, and full evidence transparency, thereby enabling rigorous comparison across systems and accelerating progress toward literature review automation serving the global research community [p1, p2, p3].
